subject: Chemistry
title: Naloxone: Reversing Opioid Overdose
description: Learn Naloxone: Reversing Opioid Overdose in one minute.

hook.text: Naloxone can reverse a fatal opioid overdose in under two minutes. It's a molecular key that unlocks death's grip.

buildup.text: Opioids bind to mu-receptors in the brain, suppressing pain but also slowing breathing to dangerous levels.

discovery.text: Naloxone has a higher affinity for mu-receptors than opioids. It pushes them off and blocks the receptor.

twist.text: It works so fast that patients wake up in immediate withdrawal—confused, sick, and sometimes combative.

climax.text: Naloxone only lasts 30–90 minutes. If the opioid is longer-acting, the overdose can return after naloxone wears off.

punchline.text: A molecule that outcompetes opioids at their own receptor. Minutes from death to awake.

quiz.question: How does naloxone reverse an opioid overdose?
options: It outcompetes opioids at mu-receptors and blocks their effect
It breaks down opioid molecules in the bloodstream
It stimulates the lungs to breathe faster
